title: Neural network quantum states in the grand canonical ensemble
authors: Anton Hul, Matija Medvidović, Juan Carrasquilla
abstract: Variational Monte Carlo calculations have recently reached state-of-the-art accuracy in the approximation of ground state properties of quantum many-body systems. Making use of flexible neural quantum states and automatic differentiation has bypassed traditional computational obstacles such as reliance on basis sets. In this paper, we propose a neural quantum state architecture capable of representing symmetric bosonic wavefunctions in Fock space, enabling the study of systems with variable particle number. By supplementing our variational state with Monte Carlo sampling and geometric optimization, we demonstrate competitive variational energies across an array of one- and two-dimensional systems, converging to the physical boson number under a set chemical potential. Our approach enables accurate estimates of one-body reduced density matrices, opening access to observables such as condensate fractions and radial density profiles from first principles. Our method opens the door to numerical predictions of key measurable quantities in practical grand canonical systems.
summary: The authors present a new Transformer-based neural network architecture designed to simulate bosonic systems in the grand canonical ensemble. This method allows for particle number fluctuations driven by a chemical potential, enabling the study of density profiles and condensate fractions from first principles. The approach demonstrates state-of-the-art accuracy for both 1D and 2D many-body models.
main_problem: Extending Neural Network Quantum States (NQS) from fixed particle number ensembles to the grand canonical ensemble to simulate bosonic systems with variable particle numbers.
main_result: The proposed Transformer-based architecture achieves highly accurate ground-state energies and reproduces exact analytical results for 1D and 2D bosonic models, outperforming previous Deep Sets architectures.
method: A Transformer-based ansatz using masked multi-head self-attention and Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) with Metropolis-Hastings sampling in Fock space.
model_or_system: Bosonic quantum field theories in the grand canonical ensemble, specifically including the 1D Lieb-Liniger and Calogero-Sutherland models, and 2D versions of the Calogero-Sutherland and harmonically trapped bosonic systems.
key_observables: Ground-state energy, average particle number, one-body reduced density matrix (OBDM), condensate fraction, and particle number density profiles.
important_parameters: Chemical potential (mu), interaction strength (g), particle number fluctuations, and the use of a masking mechanism for variable N.
main_assumptions: The architecture assumes the state can be represented as a direct sum of n-particle sectors and neglects certain three-body divergence terms in the 2D Jastrow factor.
figures_summary: Figure 1 and 2 show the Transformer architecture and MHA mechanism; Figures 3-5 display particle density and OBDM for various models; Figure 8 shows optimization convergence of energy and variance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem of GCE simulation, details the Transformer-based neural network architecture and physical constraints, presents benchmarking against 1D and 2D models, and concludes with performance comparisons and scalability discussions.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful new numerical tool for simulating realistic cold atom and photonic systems where particle number is not fixed, bridging the gap between NQS and grand canonical physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Overview of the TF based neural network ansatz, highlighting the masked multi-head attention (MHA) mecha- nism. The architecture is trained to approximate the ground state in the GCE using VMC optimization.

FIG. 2. Schematic representation of the TF architecture ansatz. The particle positions Rn = {ri}n i=1 are input to evaluate the corresponding n-particle wavefunction φn(Rn), from which the full state |Ψ⟩can be calculated as a direct sum.

FIG. 3. Particle number density n(x) obtained from the con- verged ansatzes for the one-dimensional Lieb–Liniger model with g = 10 and g = 106. The exact particle number density is shown as a black line. The standard deviation is within the linewidth.

FIG. 4. One-body density matrix ρ(x) from the converged ansatzes applied to the one-dimensional Calogero–Sutherland model with g = 5 and g = 30. The exact one-body density matrix is shown as a black line. The standard deviation is within the linewidth.

FIG. 5. Particle number density profile n(r) obtained from the converged ansatzes for the two-dimensional Calogero– Sutherland model with g = 2.0 and g = 5.0. The exact par- ticle number density is shown as a black line. The standard deviation is indicated by the shaded region.
